nativeRange.habitat: Cold, clear freshwater lakes and canal systems with muddy bottoms and aquatic vegetation, at high elevation (around 2,200 m above sea level).

decorPreferences: fine sand substrate (grain size <1 mm) or bare-bottom only
smooth caves, PVC pipes, or terracotta hides for security
shaded areas; bright lighting is not required
floating or silk plants tolerated; live plants are often uprooted
low-flow filtration with a baffled or spray-bar outflow

diet.notes: Axolotls have been documented consuming earthworms, blackworms, bloodworms, sinking carnivore pellets, small pieces of fish, and shrimp; live nightcrawlers are widely considered a staple of the captive diet. Adults are generally fed every 1–2 days. Feeder fish are best avoided, as they are commonly associated with parasite transmission and scale-related gut irritation.

goodWith: conspecifics of similar adult size (with caution; juveniles are cannibalistic)
avoidWith: tropical fish requiring warmer water (incompatible temperature)
fin-nipping or nippy fish (axolotl gills are easily damaged)
small fish or shrimp (will be eaten)
non-quarantined frogs or other amphibians (chytrid transmission risk)
snails and gravel substrates (impaction risk if swallowed)

imageCandidates.notes: Ruben Undheim, 5184x3456. Leucistic axolotl on fine sand, tack-sharp face with vivid pink feathery external gills and pale body; striking hobby-standard look on correct sand substrate.
LoKiLeCh, 2178x1215. Wild-type (dark olive mottled) axolotl, whole animal sharp and well-lit, feathery gills visible; shows the other major morph for variety.
Amandasofiarana, 1920x1280. Leucistic axolotl full side profile, whole body in frame and in focus, bright red-pink external gills; iconic look.

summary: The Axolotl is a fully aquatic freshwater salamander native to the lakes near Mexico City, and one of the most popular amphibians in the aquarium hobby. It requires cold water, fine sand or a bare bottom, and careful handling — the skin is highly sensitive and the species is prone to gut impaction if it swallows gravel.
careNotes: The Axolotl (Ambystoma mexicanum) is a coldwater, fully aquatic salamander. Unlike most salamanders, it retains its feathery external gills throughout its life and never undergoes metamorphosis into a land-dwelling form — a condition known as neoteny. Tanks are generally advised to be maintained between 14 and 22°C; temperatures above 25°C are strongly associated with stress, immune suppression, and bacterial or fungal infections. Substrate choice is critical: fine sand with a grain size under 1 mm, or a bare bottom, is the only safe option, as axolotls feed by suction and readily ingest substrate alongside food. Gravel, pebbles, and small stones have been repeatedly documented as causes of fatal gastrointestinal impaction. The skin is permeable and considered highly sensitive — copper-based medications, soap residues on hands and equipment, untreated tap water, and most fish medications should be avoided. Like most amphibians, axolotls are susceptible to chytrid fungi (Batrachochytrium dendrobatidis and B. salamandrivorans) — microscopic skin pathogens responsible for widespread amphibian declines worldwide. Co-housing with non-quarantined frogs, newts, or other amphibians is strongly discouraged, and a quarantine period of at least 30 days is generally advised for any new arrival. The species is listed as Critically Endangered in the wild (IUCN) and on CITES Appendix II; the captive trade, however, is supplied entirely by long-established captive-bred lines. These lines encompass a wide range of color morphs — wild-type, leucistic, golden albino, white albino, melanoid, copper, axanthic, piebald, GFP, mosaic, and chimera — all of which share the same care requirements. Lifespan in well-kept aquariums has been reported at 10–15 years, with some individuals living substantially longer.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding is generally considered moderately difficult, though it has been achieved reliably in stable coldwater aquariums. Sexual maturity is typically reached at around 6–18 months. Cold-cycling — dropping the tank to roughly 10–14°C for several weeks, then allowing it to warm slightly — is commonly used to induce spawning. Males deposit spermatophores (small packet-shaped structures containing sperm) on the tank floor or decor; females retrieve them with the cloaca, and fertilization is internal. Eggs are subsequently laid on plants or smooth surfaces and hatch in roughly 14 days at 24°C, with cooler temperatures extending that timeline. Juveniles are strongly cannibalistic and must be separated by size as soon as possible; larvae are generally started on live baby brine shrimp or microworms.

primary.name: Caudata Culture
primary.notes: Primary source for tank dimensions, substrate impaction warning, adult size, diet, native range, and breeding timeline.

additional.name: Axolotl.org
additional.notes: Secondary source for taxonomy, native range, color morphs, and reaffirmation of coldwater requirement and substrate impaction risk. Used the Housing sub-page for tank/decor/filtration detail.

additional.name: Axolotl Nerd — Water Requirements
additional.notes: Tertiary source for pH (6.5–8.0 tolerable, 7.4–7.6 ideal) and temperature tolerance band (15–23°C). Caudata and Axolotl.org do not state pH/hardness numerically; this source plus Swell UK provided the gH 7–14 / kH 3–8 ranges.

additional.name: Swell UK — Axolotl Care Sheet
additional.notes: Cross-check for adult size (20–30 cm), lifespan (10–15 years), water hardness (gH 7–14, kH 3–8), and recommended adult tank length (90–120 cm). Tank-size recommendation here is larger than Caudata's; we biased upward to 75 L / 75 cm minimum per the playbook's err-larger rule.

amphibian.breedingNotes: Cold-cycling — dropping the tank temperature to roughly 10–14°C for a period of several weeks, then allowing it to warm slightly — is commonly used to induce spawning. Males deposit spermatophores (small packet-shaped structures containing sperm) on the tank floor or decor; females retrieve them with the cloaca and fertilization is internal. Eggs are subsequently laid on plants or smooth surfaces and hatch in roughly 14 days at 24°C, with cooler temperatures extending that timeline. Juveniles are strongly cannibalistic and must be separated by size as soon as possible.
